hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Midpoint Review -- How Far You Have Come
lesson.menu_desc: A midpoint review of your journey: past, present, and future together -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 8 -- Midpoint Review
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 8 -- Midpoint Review

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is your teacher. I wanted to leave you a quick note before our midpoint class. I am really proud of your progress. Six months ago, you were nervous in every meeting. Now you make small talk, you follow fast speakers, and you handle a whole negotiation in English. That is a real breakthrough. In our next class, we are going to take stock together and set your goals for the second half. Come ready to talk about your strengths and one area you want to improve. See you soon, and keep up the great work.

Welcome to today's short segment on learning. Here is a question every learner should ask at the middle of a course: how far have I come? Research shows that people who pause to review their progress stay motivated far longer than those who do not. This is called self-assessment, and it is a powerful tool. The method is simple. Write down three things you can do now that you could not do at the start. Then name one strength and one area for improvement. Finally, set one clear goal for the next stage. Do not wait until the end of your journey to celebrate. Take stock at the halfway point, and you will finish stronger.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the feeling of being sure of yourself
to stay at the same level as something
something you still need to work on

paras: Every long journey has a halfway point, and it is one of the most valuable moments of all. At the start, everything is new and progress feels fast. Near the end, the goal is clear and motivation returns. But in the middle, many learners lose energy. They stop noticing how far they have come. The secret of successful people is simple: at the midpoint, they stop and take stock.

Taking stock means looking honestly at three things. First, the past: what could you not do before that you can do now? Six months ago, a fast phone call felt impossible; today, you keep up with native speakers. Second, the present: what are you working on right now? Maybe your fluency is growing while your pronunciation still needs work. Third, the future: what is your next milestone?

This honest self-assessment does something powerful. It turns a vague feeling of 'I am not good enough' into a clear map of real progress and specific areas for improvement. Confidence does not come from being perfect. It comes from seeing, with your own eyes, how much you have already achieved -- and knowing exactly where you are going next.
reading.source: Adapted from a language-learning guide, The Confident Speaker

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Most learners give up at the halfway point and should stop studying.
At the midpoint, taking honest stock of your progress builds real confidence.
Confidence only comes from speaking perfectly, with no mistakes.

items: According to the text, the halfway point is one of the most valuable moments.
The first paragraph says it is one of the most valuable moments of all.
The text says most learners gain energy in the middle of a journey.
It says many learners lose energy in the middle and stop noticing their progress.
Taking stock means looking at the past, the present, and the future.
The second paragraph describes these three things.
The text says confidence comes from being perfect.
It says confidence comes from seeing how much you have already achieved, not from being perfect.

and review your journey. Andrea can see clear

gapfill.text: Notice the time signals: 'six months ago' needs the past simple (started), 'right now' needs the present continuous (am improving), and 'next month' needs a future form (am going to present).

bank.label: Useful language for reflecting on progress
items: Six months ago, I could not do this.
Right now, I am working on my fluency.
I have made real progress this year.
My main strength is small talk.
My main area for improvement is pronunciation.
Next, I am going to present at a fair.
This is an important milestone for me.
I am taking stock of my journey.

lf.text: Match the time to the tense: 'six months ago' -> past simple (started); 'right now' / 'today' -> present continuous (am improving, am taking); 'next month' -> future (am going to present).

items: Describe one thing you could NOT do six months ago but can do now. Use the past simple and the present.
Say what you are working on right now, using the present continuous.
Explain your plan for the second half of the program, using 'going to'.
Take stock: name one strength and one area for improvement in full sentences.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = halfway point, 2 = take stock, 3 = progress, 4 = strength, 5 = confidence.
Past: 'Six months ago, I started to network in English.'
Present: 'Right now, I am improving my fluency.'
Future: 'Next month, I am going to present at a fair in Berlin.'
answerkey.note: The time word tells you the tense: ago -> past simple; right now -> present continuous; next -> going to.
